coredata.dc:title: Yellow and green-emissive N‑containing carbon dots-integrated bio-MOF: A highly sensitive sensing platform for anthrax biomarkers-picolinic and dipicolinic acids
coredata.dc:creator: Dashtian, Kheibar
Norouzi, Solmaz
Zare-Dorabei, Rouholah
Karimian, Mahsa
coredata.dc:description: The intentional release of Bacillus anthracis spores through aerosols or contaminated materials poses a serious threat to both individuals and populations. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop highly sensitive and rapid techniques for detecting Bacillus anthracis or its biomarkers, picolinic acid (PA) and dipicolinic acid (DPA), to effectively prevent and control potential outbreaks. In this study, we present a promising new approach where green and yellow emissive N‑doped carbon dots (NCDs) are integrated into bio-MOFs enriched with functional groups based on adenine (Ad) and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC) linkers. This integrated system exhibits remarkable stability, high sensitivity, and selectivity, making it an exceptional fluorescent biosensor capable of detecting PA and DPA in a linear range of 5.0 to 140.0 µM and 0.5 to 75.0 µM, respectively. The unprecedentedly low detection limits of 1.65 and 0.16 µM for PA and DPA showcase the sensor's remarkable performance. Through the successful practical application of the sensor, we have been able to rapidly and accurately analyze spiked samples of DPA and PA in urine and water, yielding excellent results and demonstrating the feasibility of this approach. Moreover, our low-cost and user-friendly visual fluorescent sensor enables preliminary qualitative analysis of DPA and PA using the naked eye. The potential of this sensor as a diagnostic system for Anthrax monitoring is highly promising and warrants further exploration for its significant contribution to disease surveillance and control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacillus anthracis
Fluorescent biosensor
g/y-carbon dots
Bio-metal–organic framework
Picolinic and dipicolinic acids

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Zn-BTC/GNCDs and Zn-BTC/YNCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Iran University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Iran University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Research Council of the Iran University of Science and Technology .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra (a) and XRD pattern (b) of as-prepared samples.
FE-SEM images of Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF sample (a-c) and TEM images of GNCDs (d-f) Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs samples (g-i) YNCDs (j-i) and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs (m-o) at different magnifications.
Particle size distribution of YNCDs and GNCDs (a) and zeta potential of the YNCDs, GNCDs, Zn-BTC/YNCDs, Zn-BTC/GNCDs and Zn-BTC samples (b).

Fluorescence spectra of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs (a, d), corresponding calibration curves (b, e), and CIE chromaticity coordinates (c, f) for various concentrations of DPA and PA at pH 9 for a reaction time of 5 min and digital photographs with color differential maps of the probes captured in the presence of different concentrations of DPA (g) and PA (f).

Fluorescence spectra of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs in the presence of different species (a, b) and the corresponding fluorescence ratios to the blank signal for all species (c, d) for DPA and PA, respectively.
(a) Synthetic route for the synthesis of GNCDs and YNCDs and (b) sensing response mechanism of Zn-BTC/Ad-CDs in the presence of DPA and PA as biomarkers of anthrax spores.

head.ce:title: Yellow and green-emissive N‑containing carbon dots-integrated bio-MOF: A highly sensitive sensing platform for anthrax biomarkers-picolinic and dipicolinic acids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry & Micro and Nano Extraction, Department of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran 16846-13114, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry & Micro and Nano Extraction
Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry & Micro and Nano Extraction, Department of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran 16846-13114, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The intentional release of Bacillus anthracis spores through aerosols or contaminated materials poses a serious threat to both individuals and populations. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop highly sensitive and rapid techniques for detecting Bacillus anthracis or its biomarkers, picolinic acid (PA) and dipicolinic acid (DPA), to effectively prevent and control potential outbreaks. In this study, we present a promising new approach where green and yellow emissive N‑doped carbon dots (NCDs) are integrated into bio-MOFs enriched with functional groups based on adenine (Ad) and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC) linkers. This integrated system exhibits remarkable stability, high sensitivity, and selectivity, making it an exceptional fluorescent biosensor capable of detecting PA and DPA in a linear range of 5.0 to 140.0 µM and 0.5 to 75.0 µM, respectively. The unprecedentedly low detection limits of 1.65 and 0.16 µM for PA and DPA showcase the sensor's remarkable performance. Through the successful practical application of the sensor, we have been able to rapidly and accurately analyze spiked samples of DPA and PA in urine and water, yielding excellent results and demonstrating the feasibility of this approach. Moreover, our low-cost and user-friendly visual fluorescent sensor enables preliminary qualitative analysis of DPA and PA using the naked eye. The potential of this sensor as a diagnostic system for Anthrax monitoring is highly promising and warrants further exploration for its significant contribution to disease surveillance and control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection and quantification of biologically significant species play a crucial role in combating infections and diseases that impact living systems [1,2]. Anthrax, caused by the bacterium Bacillus anthracis, is an acute infectious disease and has been identified as a potential biological warfare agent. The deliberate dissemination of Bacillus anthracis spores in various forms, such as aerosols or contaminated materials, underscores the critical importance of developing highly sensitive and rapid techniques for detecting Bacillus anthracis or its major biomarkers, picolinic acid (PA) and dipicolinic acid (DPA) [3]. These biomarkers are located in the cytoplasm and core area of the spores. The swift and accurate detection of these markers is essential in preventing and controlling potential outbreaks [4,5]. Numerous methods have been developed for detecting PA and DPA, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), electrochemical detection, colorimetric assay, high-pressure liquid chromatography, immunoassay, and fluorescent detection [6–10]. Among these techniques, fluorescent detection stands out as a simple, cost-effective, highly sensitive, selective, rapid and high throughput method [10]. It has gained considerable attention from researchers due to its remarkable capabilities in detecting and quantifying PA, DPA, and other biologically important species [11,12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanocarbon-based materials with exceptional properties, including low toxicity, unique optical performance, good biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness [13–15]. CDs provide several advantages over traditional fluorescent materials, such as high photonic quantum yield, photobleaching resistance, and superior water solubility [16]. Additionally, the surface of fluorescent CDs contains numerous functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino, enabling interactions with various metal ions and making them highly remarkable for various applications [17–19]. However, a drawback of using surface-modified stabilizing molecules with CDs is that during aggressive reactions, these molecules are more prone to desorption from the CDs' surface, leading to nanoparticle agglomeration and precipitation [20]. As an alternative approach, the stability of CDs materials can be enhanced by combining them with porous materials, effectively suppressing electron transition and cavitation [21,22]. The ample surface area of porous materials also provides numerous binding sites for anchoring quantum dots. Therefore, an intriguing avenue of exploration involves developing assays by incorporating luminescent materials with excellent luminescent properties into nano-porous materials[19,23]. In this regard, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are particularly attractive due to their hybrid nature of metal ions or clusters and organic ligands [24–26]. Indeed, one of the attractive features of MOFs is their ability to offer tunable pore sizes, ranging from 1 to 10 nm [27–29]. This characteristic provides abundant opportunities to effectively confine CDs and other multifunctional guest molecules within their nano-sized pores. The moderate number of binding sites within MOFs significantly augments their adaptability in accommodating a wide range of guest species [30–33]. Indeed, the incorporation of mixed ligands in MOFs with a linker possessing multiple functional groups is a highly advantageous strategy [34–36]. The introduction of a second bio-ligand alongside the primary ligand, each with different functional groups, offers the flexibility to tailor the MOF's structure and properties to suit various applications [37]. Moreover, selecting a linker with numerous functional groups enhances the MOF's binding capabilities, providing ample opportunities for CD molecule confinement and interaction. This can be achieved through various methods, including anchoring, encapsulation, cracking, impregnation, and post-modification [23,38]. Herein, we have achieved the successful synthesis of yellow emissive N‑containing CDs (YNCDs) and green emissive N‑containing CDs (GNCDs). These N‑containing CDs were then incorporated into Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF, resulting in the creation of two fluorescent biosensors. These biosensors have demonstrated exceptional accuracy, selectivity, and sensitivity in detecting PA and DPA. The promising performance of these biosensors opens up practical applications for the detection of PA and DPA in various environmental and biological samples. Their high accuracy and sensitivity make them a valuable choice for such analytical purposes.

The following chemical were purchased and used without further purification: pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (DPA-C7H5NO4) (Sigma-Aldrich), pyridine-2,5-dicarboxylic acid (C7H5NO4) (Sigma-Aldrich), adenine (C5H5N5) (Sigma-Aldrich), 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC) (Sigma-Aldrich), 1,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BDC- C8H6O4) (Sigma-Aldrich), Aspartic acid (ASA-C4H7NO4) (Sigma-Aldrich), L-tyrosine (L-ty-C6H11NO3) (Sigma-Aldrich), L-Isoleucine (L-iso-C6H13NO2) (Sigma-Aldrich), hexamethylenediamine (HMDA- C6H16N2) (Sigma-Aldrich), ethanol (96 %, Merck), Dimethylformamide (DMF) (99 %, Merck) adenine(C5H5N5) (Sigma-Aldrich), polyvidone (Sigma-Aldrich), Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2) (Sigma-Aldrich), Sodium formate (HCOONa) (Sigma-Aldrich), 1,3,5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (C9H6O6) (Sigma-Aldrich), Nitric acid (HNO3) (Sigma-Aldrich), o-Phenylenediamine (C6H8N2) (Sigma-Aldrich) and l-Tyrosine (C9H11NO3) (Sigma-Aldrich).

The fluorescence spectra were analyzed using a molecular fluorescence spectrofluorometer (FL-Ar-2015). The pH values were measured with a pH Meter (Mettler Toledo- S220-BIO). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer- Spectrum 100). Absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–vis Spectrophotometer (PG Instruments-plus T80). X-ray diffraction analysis was performed on an X-ray powder diffractometer (Philips-PW1730). For Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) measurements, an FE-SEM ZEISS-sigma 300 was used. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a TEM Philips- EM 208S. Zeta potential measurements were carried out using a Malvern-Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument from Malvern Instruments, UK.

Scheme 1 a presents the synthesis of y-CDs and g-CDs. For g-CDs preparation, a solution of 0.5 mmol of o-phenylenediamine was prepared by dissolving it in 10 mL of ethanol. The solution was then transferred into a PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature [39]. Regarding the synthesis of y-CDs, a solution containing 1.0 mmol of o-PDs and 1.0 mmol of L-Ty was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture underwent sonication for 15 min and was later transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined autoclave. The autoclave was then heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After completion, the autoclave was automatically cooled to room temperature, and the solution was gently removed. To clean the reactor, it was soaked in methanol and sonicated for 30 min [40].

Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF was prepared using the previously reported ultrasound-assisted solvothermal method [41]. Initially, 0.08 g of adenine (C5H5N5) was dissolved in 8 mL of warm DMF while stirring. in another vial, 0.11 g of zinc chloride and 0.04 g of sodium formate (HCOONa) were dissolved in 4 mL of DMF and stirred for 10 min. followed by the addition of 0.12 g BTC. Finally, nitric acid (0.2 mmol) in 3 mL of H2O was added. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 130 °C for 24 h. After the completion of the reaction, the resulting white crystals were filtered and washed several times with DMF to remove any impurities from the final product. The pure crystals were subjected to further activation by heating in ethanol at 78 °C. After activation, the solvent was filtered out, and the product was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 10 h.

To synthesize Zn-BTC/Ad/y-CDs, a solution was prepared by dissolving 0.3 mL of yellow quantum dots in 60 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, 3.2 mg of Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF was added to the solution, and the mixture was stirred for 2 h. The same procedure was repeated for the syntheses of Zn-BTC/Ad-g-CDs (See Scheme 1b).

The detection procedures for the Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs probes were conducted as follows: Initially, Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs were placed in separate tubes, and samples ranging from 5 μM to 140 μM for Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs and from 0.5 μM to 140 μM for Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs were added. The total volume of the mixture was adjusted to 1 mL using deionized water, ensuring that the concentration of the probes remained at 2 mg/L. The reaction was allowed to proceed for a specific period at room temperature with continuous shaking. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. (For complete details on the DPA and DA sensing steps, refer to Scheme 1b). To evaluate the sensor's selectivity towards PA and DPA, other compounds with similar structures were also tested using the same experimental procedure. To ensure the reliability and stability of the results, three replicate assays were performed.

The FT-IR spectra (a) and XRD patterns (b) were analyzed for various samples, including Zn-BTC/Ad, GNCDs, YNCDs, Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs, and Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs. The FT-IR spectrum of Zn-BTC/Ad displayed distinctive peaks, such as Zn-O stretching at 725 cm−1, C-H bending vibrations at 1006 cm−1, C-C stretching vibrations at 1103 cm−1, C-O stretching of carboxylate at 1340 cm−1, CO stretching at 1630 cm−1, and –COO– bending at 1760 cm−1 [42]. Moreover, the band within the 3300–3400 cm−1 range suggested O-H stretching vibrations from adsorbed water. The N-H group of adenines exhibited stretching frequencies ranging from 3117 to 3338 cm−1 [41]. In the FT-IR spectrum, we identified bands within the 3300–3400 cm−1 range, indicating the presence of NH2 and OH groups, along with an absorption band within the 2800–3000 cm−1 range corresponding to C-H stretching vibrations [43]. Additional peaks corresponding to functional groups like CO (1639 cm−1), N–H (1500 cm−1), CC (1270 cm−1), C–NH–C (1160 cm−1), and C–C (1030–1060 cm−1) were also observed. These peaks partially corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the bonds between C, N, and O in the aliphatic group [43,44]. The presence of polar functional groups on the CDs' surfaces enables their easy dispersion in water and loading onto MOFs, facilitating interaction with DPA and PA targets. In the FT-IR spectrum of GNCD and YNCD loaded Zn-BTC/Ad, we observed functional groups from both components, and a reduction in band intensity and broadening indicated the successful interaction of functional groups on the surface of YNCD and GNCD with Zn-BTC.

The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) for both green and yellow CDs display a broad peak spanning from 15° to 30°, indicating their amorphous nature [45]. The Pertwix model was employed to assess the successful synthesis of Zn-BTC/Ad in comparison to its simulation model, which is supported by the crystallinity information with file number 1,507,714 (Fig. 1 b) [41]. Upon the loading of GNCDs and YNCDs onto the Zn-BTC/Ad structure, the observed bands exhibited broadening, and their intensity decreased. Furthermore, the presence of CDs induced an amorphous background, confirming the successful synthesis of Zn-BTC/YNCDs and Zn-BTC/GNCDs (Fig. 1b) [41,46,47].

The surface morphology of the synthesized samples was comprehensively examined through FE-SEM and TEM analysis, with corresponding images provided in Fig. 2 . In the FE-SEM images, the rod-plate-like structure of Zn-BTC/Ad is clearly evident (Fig. 2a-c). In the case of GNCDs, their images depict quasi-spherical particles resembling CDs in the size range of 5–15 nm, which are tightly integrated and aggregated, forming a wire-like structure (Fig. 2d–f). After the one-step loading of GNCDs onto Zn-BTC, fine dots of GNCDs become discernible on the surface of Zn-BTC (Fig. 2g–i). Regarding the YNCDs, the images vividly illustrate the core–shell structure of this compound. The core is formed through successful carbonization, with 5–10 nm quasi-spherical particles aggregated, while the shell is formed through incomplete carbonization and includes the presence of functional groups (Fig. 2j–l). Subsequently, after the one-step loading of GNCDs on Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF, fine dots of YNCDs can be observed on the Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF rod plates (Fig. 2m–o) [48]. EDS analysis of the g-CDs and y-CDs reveals the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements (See Fig. S2). This confirms the successful synthesis of the desired compound, and the chlorine impurity percentage is also indicated, which can be attributed to the raw materials used in the synthesis process.

The DLS diagrams for both yellow and green CDs reveal hydrodynamic radius values ranging from 4 to 100 nm and 10 to 100 nm, respectively, which represent the average intensity of the CDs' hydrodynamic radius (Fig. 3 a). In the case of GNCDs, the primary peaks, falling within the range of 4 to 10 nm, are attributed to the carbon dot structures. The extension of these peaks up to the limit of 100 nm can be ascribed to the accumulation of particles and the formation of nuclei with varying dimensions. In contrast, the hydrodynamic radius size of YNCDs shows an average of approximately 15 nm, indicating a relatively higher degree of particle accumulation in this type of CDs (Fig. 3a). These findings are consistent with the TEM results, which confirm the presence of particle accumulation in both types of CDs.

The zeta potential of the green and yellow CDs was estimated to be 7.77 mV and −4.75 mV, respectively. Comparatively, the zeta potential of Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF is −13.3 mV, while the potential of green and yellow CDs in the presence of Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF was obtained as −12.6 mV and −10 mV, respectively. This shift towards more positive values confirms the interaction of the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots with Zn-BTC, as shown in Fig. 3b [49].

The optical properties of YNCDs and GNCDs were examined through UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra analysis. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3a), peaks at 475 nm and 485 nm were observed for GNCDs and YNCDs, respectively, which can be attributed to the π to π* transition. For fluorescence measurements, an excitation wavelength of 395 nm was used. The normalized fluorescence spectra of GNCDs and YNCDs show fluorescence peaks at 544 nm and 555 nm, respectively (Fig. S3b). Both fluorescent carbon dots exhibited highly saturated and pure colors, as illustrated by the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram depicted in Fig. S3c and Fig. S3d, respectively.

The impact of various operational parameters on the performance of the proposed fluorescent probes was thoroughly investigated. Assay time was explored in Section S3 (Fig. S4), pH variations were studied in Section S4 (Fig. S5 and S6), and temperature effects were examined in Section S5 (Fig. S7) to optimize the sensitivity and selectivity of the probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Zn-BTC/GNCDs and Zn-BTC/YNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence titration experiments were conducted to investigate the sensing behavior of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs (Fig. 4 ). The results revealed that BTC/GNCDs selectively interacted with DPA, as evidenced by the decrease in fluorescence intensity at 544 nm with increasing DPA concentration (Fig. 4a). The linear relationship between the decrease in emission intensity and DPA concentration in the range of 5–140 μM was described by the equations (F0-F)/F0 = 0.016 [DPA] + 0.004 and (F0-F)/F0 = 0.001 [DPA] + 0.35 (Fig. 4b). The color change with increasing water content was visualized using CIE coordinates, showing a gradual shift of the Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs probe from light green to pale green (Fig. 4c). The limit of quantification (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) values were calculated as 5 μM and 1.65 μM, respectively. The repeatability of the sensor was assessed by conducting five measurements under identical experimental conditions in the presence of 20 μM DPA, yielding a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.206 %. On the other hand, the Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs probe exhibited a selective sensing response toward PA. The fluorescence intensity at 555 nm of the sensor Zn-BTC-YNCDs was observed to increase with the addition of PA (Fig. 4d). The linear relationship between the increase in emission intensity and PA concentration in the range of 0.5–75 μM was described by the equations (F − F0)/F0 = 0.004 [PA] + 0.57 and (F − F0)/F0 = 0.048 [PA] (Fig. 4e). The color change with increasing water content was illustrated using CIE coordinates, showing a gradual shift of the Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs probe from light yellow to sharp yellow (Fig. 4f). Moreover, to achieve a more thorough understanding of the visual interpretation of these easily interpretable sensors, digital photographs of the probes were captured under varying concentrations of DPA and PA. A visual color difference map between the initial and modulated fluorescence provides a clear representation. When the fluorescence of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs sensing elements remains unchanged before the addition of targets, the color difference is zero. However, upon the introduction of concentration, the differential map shifts to green for DPA (Fig. 4g) and deep blue for PA (Fig. 4h). Notably, a noticeable and distinct change in color is easily discernible without the need for specialized instruments. The LOQ and LOD values were calculated to be 0.5 μM and 0.16 μM, respectively. The repeatability of the sensor was evaluated by conducting five measurements under identical experimental conditions in the presence of 20 μM PA, resulting in a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 5.2 %. Furthermore, the results provide strong evidence that the color change can be easily discerned by the naked eye when excited at 375 nm. This feature contributes to the adaptability and practicality of their use in sensing applications.

Substances that reduce the fluorescence intensity of fluorophores are referred to as quenchers. The Stern-Volmer equation is expressed as follows: F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q], where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore in the absence of the quencher, F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of the quencher, [Q] is the concentration of DPA added to the reaction medium as the quencher, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Fig. S8a). Moreover, for the Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs probes, the fluorescence enhancement was qualitatively assessed using the following equation, fitted with the calibration curve (Fig. S8b): F/F0 = 1 + Kec[M], where [M] is the concentration of PA and Kec is the fluorescence enhancement coefficient. The calculated KSV and Kec values were found to be 0.025 × 106 1/M and 1.08 × 106 1/M for both DPA and PA. Furthermore, to evaluate the binding constant (Kb) and quenching ability of DPA when interacting with Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs, the following formula was used: log(F0 − F/F) = logKb + nlog[Q] (Fig. S8c). Here, n represents the number of connecting/interacting sites, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of the quencher, and F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of the quencher. Similarly, for PA, the Kb was calculated using the formula: log(F − F0/F0) = logKb + nlog[M] (Fig. S8d). The determined Kb values were found to be 0.018 × 106 1/M and 0.050 × 106 1/M for both DPA and PA. The findings align with the calibration curve and validate the strong interaction between Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and DPA, in contrast to the interaction between Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and PA. This difference in interaction strength can be attributed to variations in polarity, pKa, size, and shape.

The potential mechanism behind the fluorescence turn-off and turn-on phenomenon was thoroughly investigated for Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs, respectively. Fluorescence quenching can be understood through various mechanisms, such as Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the formation of ground-state complexes, and the inner filter effect (IFE) [44,50]. These mechanisms collectively contribute to explaining the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching. In this study, the absorption wavelength of DPA was determined to be 271 nm, while the dominant fluorescence emission of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs occurred at 544 nm. Since there was no spectral overlap between the absorbance of DPA and the emission of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs, FRET was ruled out as the quenching mechanism. Instead, the observed fluorescence quenching was attributed to PET. Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs acted as an electron donor, while DPA served as an electron acceptor. Upon the introduction of DPA into the Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs, DPA formed hydrogen bonds with adenine and GNCDs, and also engaged in π-π interactions with adenine, BTC linker, and GNCDs, while interacting with Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs bio-MOF through van der Waals forces. This collective binding interaction facilitated the attachment of DPA to the surface of Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF, creating an appropriate distance for PET to occur between Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and DPA. As a result, DPA accepted an electron from the excited state of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs bio-MOF. Furthermore, the mechanism of Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs in detecting PA is discussed. As mentioned earlier, YNCDs were integrated with Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF through π-π and hydrogen bonding interactions. However, the fluorescence of Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs was not greatly enhanced, partly due to the lack of fluorescence resonance energy transfer between YNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-bio-MOF [51]. The main reason for this is the presence of PET between the electron-deficient BTC/Ad and electron-rich YNCDs components. Upon the addition of PA, which is an electron-acceptor group, it formed hydrogen bonds with adenine and GNCDs, and also engaged in π-π interactions with adenine, BTC linker, and YNCDs, while interacting with Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs through Van der Waals forces. This inhibits the PET process and gradually restores the fluorescence of Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs. As a result, the presence of PA enables the enhancement of fluorescence in Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs, providing a sensitive detection mechanism for PA [52].

The selectivity of the Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs probes was meticulously assessed for their ability to detect DPA and PA, even in the presence of various interfering species, including 5–2-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, 2-pyridinecarboxylic acid, 1–4-benzenetricarboxylic acid, L-tyrosine, L-cysteine, 1–3-5-Benzene tricarboxylic acid, aspartic acid, and hexamethylenediamine, all at a concentration of 50 μM (Fig. 5 ). The results demonstrated the sensors' remarkable selectivity towards DPA and PA, with only a minor interference effect observed with a 2,5-pyridine dicarboxylic acid, likely due to their similar shape, structure, functional groups, and pKa. This finding highlights the significant potential of Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs probes as highly selective sensors for detecting DPA and PA, making them valuable tools for the detection of anthrax biomarkers.

The feasibility and practical applicability of our developed sensing assay were assessed by testing tap water and urine samples for PA and DPA detection (The details for sample preparation are provided in the supporting information file (See section S6)). The experiments were repeated three times to ensure accuracy. Additionally, a recovery test was conducted using spiked PA and DPA at different concentrations to validate the sensing assay. Satisfactory recoveries (R%) ranging from 94.8 % to 106. 6 % were obtained, as shown in Table S1. Moreover, to improve the clarity of the visual interpretation of these visually accessible sensors, Fig. S9 presents digital photographs of the probes in a urine sample, captured under different concentrations of DPA and PA.

The consistent results obtained from our method affirm its reliability as a viable alternative to conventional methods for quantitatively determining DPA and PA anthrax biomarkers in mentioned samples. The developed sensing probes exhibited satisfactory LOQ and LOD values, which are consistent with previously reported sensors for PA and DPA (Table S2). Existing literature on PA and DPA sensors often faces limitations related to specificity, selectivity, or sensitivity. However, our synthesized Zn-BTC/Ad-GNCDs and Zn-BTC/Ad-YNCDs probes have addressed these issues effectively. These probes overcome the low absorptivity and quantum yield associated with conventional fluorophores, and the incorporation of adenine and aminated quantum dots has significantly improved the specificity and selectivity of the biosensor.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized GNCDs and YNCDs, in combination with Zn-BTC/Ad bio-MOF, through solvothermal reactions, to develop highly sensitive and selective fluorescent biosensors for the detection of Bacillus anthracis or its major biomarkers, picolinic acid, and dipicolinic acid. Notably, both internal factors, such as background fluorescence, and external factors, including pH and time, had negligible or minimal impacts on the biosensor's performance. The figure of merits, including repeatability, limit of detection, and limit of linearity thoroughly assessed to evaluate the method's effectiveness in detecting DPA and PA in real water and urine samples. This proposed universal approach offers a remarkable sensing strategy, significantly enhancing the accuracy and speed of detection while avoiding false positives, which is crucial for early disease diagnosis. Overall, this study has opened up new possibilities and potential applications for this method, demonstrating its efficacy as a reliable and valuable tool in various sensing applications.

Kheibar Dashtian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Solmaz Norouzi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Rouholah Zare-Dorabei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mahsa Karimian: Data curation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Research Council of the Iran University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.149229.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
